Battista

EU External Action · Security · Global Governance

Graduate student pursuing a second Master’s degree in European Studies at KU Leuven, with a background in International Relations and Political Science from the University of Amsterdam and VUB. Currently specialising in EU external action, security and global governance, with hands-on experience supporting EU-funded projects in Brussels. Motivated to contribute to institutions and agencies working at the intersection of policy, international cooperation and strategic affairs.

Experience

Professional experience in EU policy and project coordination

EAVI — European Association for Viewers Interests
Project Development and Communication Intern
March 2026 – July 2026 · Brussels, Belgium

Based in Brussels, EAVI works across Europe to foster active citizenship in a challenging media environment. My role involves supporting the lifecycle of large-scale EU projects—from partner communication to strategic outreach—to help individuals navigate today’s digital world safely and critically.

  • Supported the coordination of EU-funded projects under Erasmus+, Horizon Europe and CERV, including partner communication and preparation of project deliverables.
  • Contributed to the organisation of events such as the European Festival of Journalism and Media Freedom, supporting workshops, panels and stakeholder engagement.
  • Drafted and supported communication and outreach activities, including social media content, project visibility and dissemination tasks.

Research

Master’s thesis projects exploring EU external action through critical lenses

✓ Completed
Universiteit van Amsterdam · 30 ECTS · 2024–2025

The European Hegemonic Project in Georgia: A Critical Discourse Analysis on the Strategic Use of EU Discourse

“How does the EU strategically deploy normative discourse in Georgia to advance both values-based and geopolitical interests?”
Critical Discourse Analysis ATLAS.ti 42 EU Documents 315 Coded Quotations 11 Codes

This thesis examines how the EU strategically deploys its normative discourse in Georgia, analysing the interplay between EU self-representation and its geopolitical ambitions within the Eastern Partnership framework. Using Critical Discourse Analysis on 42 official EU documents spanning 2004–2025, the research reveals patterns of moralisation, conditionality, and the construction of Self/Other binaries in EU communications about Georgia.

Read Full Thesis (UvA Repository)

Qualitative Data Analysis — ATLAS.ti

42
Documents
315
Quotations
11
Codes
● In Progress
KU Leuven · MEAS Programme · 2025–2026

EU Horizon and International Responsibility: Complicity in the Funding of Deathworlds in Palestine?

“To what extent does the EU, through the funding mechanisms of the Horizon programme, materially contribute to the construction of a deathworld in the Occupied Palestinian Territories?”
Legal Case Study ARIO Framework Critical Theory Horizon Europe

This thesis investigates the EU’s potential international responsibility for aiding or assisting internationally wrongful acts through the Horizon research programme. It examines how EU funding flows to Israeli entities structurally linked to the military-academic complex maintaining the occupation, applying the Articles on the Responsibility of International Organizations (ARIO) alongside critical frameworks of coloniality, legal subalternity, and necropolitics.

Supervisor: prof. dr. Stephan Keukelier
Coloniality
Structural suspension of the Palestinian as a rights-bearing subject (Huber, 2025)
Legal Subalternity
Systemic failure to enforce Palestinian rights in int’l law (Imseis, 2018)
Necropolitics
Technologically mediated deathworlds through surveillance & control (Mbembe, 2019)

Political Analysis

Why Georgians Took to the Streets: Anti-Russian Sentiment and the Tbilisi Protests

Giulio Leonardo Battista · Academic Paper · 2025

When thousands of people filled the streets of Tbilisi after Georgia’s October 2024 parliamentary elections, their anger was about more than a disputed vote. The protests quickly became a broader political statement about the country’s direction, its democratic future, and, above all, its relationship with Russia.

Waving EU flags and chanting pro-European slogans, demonstrators were not simply rejecting the victory of the ruling Georgian Dream party. They were also expressing a deeper fear: that Georgia was drifting back into Moscow’s orbit.

A History That Still Shapes the Present

To understand why Russia remains such a powerful factor in Georgian politics, it is necessary to look at the country’s post-Soviet history.

Georgia gained independence in 1991 with the collapse of the Soviet Union, but sovereignty did not mean freedom from Russian pressure. The 1990s were marked by civil war, separatist conflicts, and political instability, especially in Abkhazia and South Ossetia. Russia initially supported separatist actors and later positioned itself as a peace broker, securing a long-term military presence in these contested areas.

For many Georgians, this pattern established a familiar dynamic: Moscow presented itself as a stabilising force while in reality deepening Georgia’s dependence and vulnerability.

This tension intensified under President Mikheil Saakashvili, who came to power after the Rose Revolution in 2003. Saakashvili pushed Georgia in a distinctly pro-Western direction, seeking closer ties with both NATO and the European Union. That shift was perceived by Moscow as a direct challenge.

The result was the 2008 Russo-Georgian war, a short but deeply consequential conflict that cemented Russia’s image in Georgia not as a partner, but as an occupying power. Since then, anti-Russian sentiment has remained one of the most important undercurrents in Georgian political life.

Anti-Russian Sentiment Is Real — but It Is Complex

Public attitudes in Georgia show a clear pattern: hostility toward the Russian state is strong, while attitudes toward the Russian people are far less negative. This distinction matters. For many Georgians, the issue is not ethnic or cultural animosity, but political fear. Russia is seen as a threat because of its military occupation of Abkhazia and South Ossetia, its broader regional ambitions, and its efforts to keep Georgia inside its sphere of influence.

Survey data reflects this reality. Large majorities of Georgians describe relations with Russia as hostile, while support for the European Union remains consistently high. Across age groups and regions, EU membership is seen not only as a foreign policy goal but as a civilisational choice — one tied to democracy, sovereignty and protection from Russian domination.

The Georgian Dream’s Political Transformation

When Georgian Dream first came to power in 2012, it presented itself as broadly pro-Western, despite promising a less confrontational relationship with Russia than Saakashvili had pursued. For a time, this balancing act seemed politically sustainable.

But over the years, and especially in the run-up to the 2024 elections, the party’s direction changed in visible ways. Its rhetoric grew more anti-Western. Its campaigns increasingly relied on fear-based narratives, including claims that the West wanted to drag Georgia into war with Russia. It also embraced more authoritarian tactics, most notably through the reintroduction of the foreign influence law, widely criticised as a Georgian version of Russia’s “foreign agents” legislation.

The Election and the Breaking Point

The October 26, 2024 parliamentary election became the trigger for this broader discontent. After Georgian Dream claimed victory, the European Parliament rejected the legitimacy of the result, citing significant irregularities, including voter intimidation, media interference, vote manipulation and problems with electronic voting procedures.

Instead of seeking to restore trust, the Georgian government escalated the confrontation. It announced that it would halt discussions on EU accession until 2028. That decision struck at the heart of Georgian public opinion. For years, surveys had shown overwhelming support for EU membership, often hovering around 80 percent. In that context, suspending the integration process looked less like a policy adjustment and more like a strategic reorientation away from Europe.

Repression and Democratic Backsliding

The government’s response only deepened this perception. Security forces used aggressive dispersal tactics, arrests, beatings, tear gas and water cannons against protesters. Human rights organisations, including Amnesty International, documented widespread abuses and denounced the crackdown as a serious violation of freedom of assembly and expression.

Later, the introduction of a new restrictive law in December 2024 further criminalised protest activity and expanded the state’s ability to suppress dissent. Analysts increasingly described Georgia as moving toward autocratisation under Georgian Dream.

More Than a Protest, a Geopolitical Choice

The protests in Tbilisi revealed a fundamental divide between the Georgian government and much of Georgian society. While Georgian Dream appeared increasingly comfortable with anti-Western rhetoric, illiberal laws and a pause in EU accession, large parts of the population made clear that they wanted the opposite: closer ties with Europe, democratic accountability and distance from Russia.

Anti-Russian sentiment was therefore not just a background condition. It was one of the central forces that transformed post-election anger into sustained public protest.

Bibliography

Amnesty International. (2024a). Georgia: Police committing shocking human rights violations amid ongoing crackdown on protesters.
Amnesty International. (2024b). Georgia: Protesters face arrests, abuse, and denial of fair trial rights.
Amnesty International. (2025). Georgia: Authorities using draconian new laws to crack down on dissent.
Bilanishvili, G., et al. (2021). Attitudes and Perceptions Towards Russia in Georgia. Georgian Foundation for Strategic and International Studies.
Caucasus Barometer 2024 Georgia. (2024). Caucasus Research Resource Center.
Dickinson, P. (2021). The 2008 Russo-Georgian War: Putin’s Green Light. Atlantic Council.
European Parliament. (2024). Parliament Calls for New Elections in Georgia.
Grozovski, B. (2024). Russia’s Top-Down Capture of Georgia. Wilson Center.
Kakachia, K., et al. (2024). Elections 2024: What Is Georgian Dream’s Dream for Georgia? Georgian Institute of Politics.
Kandelaki, S. (2024). From Elections to Elections: Transformation of the Georgian Ruling Political Party. Georgian Institute of Politics.
Kofman, M. (2018). The August War, Ten Years On. War on the Rocks.
Mills, C. (2024). The Impact of Georgia’s ‘Foreign Influence’ Law. House of Commons Library.
Panchulidze, E. & Youngs, R. (2025). The Source of Georgia’s Democratic Resilience. Journal of Democracy, 36(1), 123–134.
Spetschinsky, L. & Bolgova, I. V. (2014). Post-Soviet or Post-Colonial? ERIS, 1(3), 110–122.
Tavkhelidze, T. (2024). The Rationale for Reintroducing the Foreign Agent Bill in Georgia. Caucasus Analytical Digest, 137, 8–13.
Security & Defence

Cheap Drones, Expensive Consequences: How Low-Tech UAVs Are Reshaping Modern Warfare

Giulio Leonardo Battista · Academic Paper · 2023

In recent years, drones have moved from being niche military technology to one of the most influential tools in modern warfare. Once associated mainly with advanced military powers, unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) are now accessible to a wide range of actors — from national armies to militant groups.

What makes this shift particularly striking is that some of the most impactful drones are not the highly sophisticated military models often discussed in defence circles. Instead, they are frequently cheap, commercially available devices that can be easily modified for military purposes.

The Rise of the “Simple” Drone

The rapid proliferation of drones is one of the defining developments in 21st-century warfare. The smallest category — often weighing less than 150 kilograms — has become particularly important in recent conflicts.

The appeal of these systems is straightforward. Compared to traditional aircraft, drones are far cheaper to produce, maintain and operate. They can also be deployed without putting a pilot at risk. Commercial drones offer an additional advantage: accessibility. With relatively simple modifications — attaching cameras, explosives or targeting systems — they can be transformed into reconnaissance tools or one-way attack drones.

This cost-per-effect logic has reshaped military innovation. Rather than focusing solely on advanced technologies, many actors now prioritise quantity and adaptability, deploying large numbers of inexpensive drones capable of overwhelming defences.

The growing demand has also reshaped global arms markets. Countries such as Turkey, China and Iran have become major drone exporters, supplying states that cannot easily purchase systems from traditional suppliers. In this sense, drone technology has also become a geopolitical tool, expanding diplomatic ties and influence.

Drones in the Hands of Non-State Actors

The accessibility of drone technology has also empowered militant organisations. Groups such as Hamas have increasingly relied on low-tech drones to support their operations. One of the most striking advantages lies in the imbalance between the cost of offence and defence. A commercial drone may cost only a few hundred or a few thousand dollars, while intercepting it often requires sophisticated air-defence missiles that cost tens of thousands of dollars each.

The October 2023 Hamas attack on Israel demonstrated how drones can be integrated into broader hybrid strategies. During the assault, drones equipped with explosives were reportedly used to target Israeli surveillance systems and military positions near the border. At the same time, thousands of rockets were launched into Israeli territory, creating confusion and overwhelming defensive systems.

Ukraine’s Drone Battlefield

The war between Russia and Ukraine has shown how these technologies can reshape large-scale interstate conflict. The war has seen an unprecedented level of drone use, both in terms of quantity and diversity.

For Ukraine, these drones have played a crucial role in compensating for Russia’s greater conventional military capabilities. Simple UAVs costing only a few thousand dollars have been used to destroy tanks and armoured vehicles worth millions.

The war has also produced a unique form of military innovation driven by civil society. Ukrainian forces have received large numbers of drones through crowdfunding initiatives and private donations — sometimes referred to as “dronations.” These efforts have allowed civilians, hobbyists and international supporters to contribute directly to Ukraine’s drone supply.

Another notable development is the increasing use of swarm tactics. Large numbers of inexpensive drones can be launched simultaneously to confuse or overwhelm air defences.

The Moral Dilemma of Drone Warfare

While drones offer clear tactical advantages, their growing use raises important ethical concerns. Some scholars argue that drone warfare challenges traditional principles of just war, particularly the idea that combatants should face comparable risks.

Others worry that drones lower the political threshold for the use of force. Because they reduce the risk to soldiers, governments may be more willing to conduct military operations that would otherwise face greater political scrutiny.

The United States’ drone campaigns in countries such as Pakistan, Yemen and Somalia illustrate these concerns. Many strikes were conducted with limited transparency, and estimates suggest that thousands of people were killed, including civilians.

Despite these concerns, international regulation remains limited. Export controls such as the Missile Technology Control Regime mainly apply to large armed drones with long ranges and heavy payloads. Many smaller systems now widely used in conflicts fall outside these restrictions.

A Changing Face of War

The widespread adoption of cheap drones marks a profound shift in how wars are fought. Their affordability, adaptability and accessibility allow weaker actors to challenge stronger militaries, narrowing the technological gap between them. What began as a niche military technology has quickly become a central feature of modern conflict. The drone revolution is already underway — and it is redefining the future of warfare.

Bibliography

Chávez, K. & Swed, O. (2023). Emulating underdogs: Tactical drones in the Russia–Ukraine war. Contemporary Security Policy, 44(4), 592–605.
Custers, B. (2016). The Future of Drone Use. T.M.C. Asser Press.
Dostri, O. (2023). Hamas’s October 2023 Attack on Israel. Military Review.
Enemark, C. (2023). Moralities of Drone Violence. Edinburgh University Press.
Hall, J. M. (2023). Just War Contra Drone Warfare. Conatus, 8(2), 217–239.
Kreps, S., et al. (2018). Drone proliferation in the twenty-first century. Oxford Handbook of International Security, 577–592.
Kunertova, D. (2023a). Drones Have Boots. Contemporary Security Policy, 44(4), 576–591.
Kunertova, D. (2023b). The War in Ukraine Shows the Game-Changing Effect of Drones Depends on the Game. Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists, 79(2), 95–102.
Samaan, J. L. (2023). Drone Warfare and the Changing Dynamics of Conflict in the Middle East.
Sports & Regulation

From Betting to Crypto: How Gambling Is Rebranding Itself in European Football

Giulio Leonardo Battista · Academic Paper · 2023

Over the past two decades, gambling companies have become some of the most visible sponsors in professional football. From shirt logos to pitch-side advertising boards, betting brands have deeply embedded themselves in the sport’s commercial ecosystem. But the growing presence of betting companies has also triggered public health concerns and political debates across Europe.

Football’s Gambling Boom

In England, gambling companies quickly became dominant sponsors. By 2015, the industry was spending more than £115 million annually on television advertising related to sports betting. During the 2022–2023 Premier League season, every club had at least one betting partner, and eight teams displayed gambling sponsors on their match shirts.

Facing growing criticism, Premier League clubs later agreed to remove betting companies from the front of shirts beginning in the 2025–2026 season. However, gambling brands remain allowed on shirt sleeves, advertising boards, stadium screens, and digital media.

The Italian Paradox

In 2018 the Italian government introduced the Dignity Decree, which prohibited most forms of gambling advertising, including sponsorships during sporting events. On paper, the law appeared to be one of the strictest in Europe. In practice, however, gambling companies quickly found ways to remain present.

One common tactic involved the use of affiliated “sister companies.” A gambling operator might launch a seemingly separate brand — for example, transforming a betting site into a sports news or informational platform. A recent example involved Inter Milan and the gambling group Betsson through its subsidiary StarCasinò.

The Rise of “Gamblified” Finance

At the same time gambling sponsors have faced stricter regulation, cryptocurrency and financial trading companies have rapidly expanded across football. This shift reflects a broader phenomenon known as gamblification — the integration of gambling-like mechanics into non-gambling activities.

In gamblified systems, financial products are designed to mimic the excitement, risk and reward structure of gambling. Trading platforms, cryptocurrency exchanges and digital tokens often incorporate elements such as high-frequency trading, volatility, and the promise of large profits.

Cryptocurrency and Risk Behaviour

Unlike traditional financial markets, cryptocurrency exchanges operate around the clock and often lack strong regulatory oversight. The extreme volatility of digital assets encourages speculative trading and short-term profit seeking.

Studies have found that many cryptocurrency traders share characteristics with traditional gamblers, including high risk tolerance, impulsive decision-making, and susceptibility to psychological biases such as the fear of missing out. Research also shows significant overlap between cryptocurrency trading and gambling behaviour.

Fan Tokens and the Financialisation of Fandom

Fan tokens are digital assets that allow supporters to participate in club-related decisions, such as voting on minor team matters or accessing exclusive fan experiences. Italian clubs including Juventus, Inter and AC Milan have launched their own tokens, generating millions of euros in revenue.

However, critics argue that fan tokens share several characteristics with speculative financial assets. Their prices can fluctuate dramatically depending on team performance or market sentiment, exposing fans to financial risks they may not fully understand.

By the 2023–2024 season, nearly half of Serie A clubs had partnerships with crypto exchanges or trading platforms. This shift suggests that restrictions on gambling advertising may not reduce exposure to risky financial products — it may simply redirect sponsorship toward new industries operating under weaker regulatory frameworks.

The Future of Gambling in Football

Football’s commercial ecosystem continues to evolve rapidly. Where football once promoted betting companies directly, it now increasingly promotes financial products that mimic gambling’s psychological appeal. Understanding this shift will be crucial for policymakers, regulators and researchers trying to protect fans while maintaining the commercial sustainability of modern sport.

Bibliography

Andrade, M. & Newall, P. W. (2023). Cryptocurrencies as gamblified financial assets and cryptocasinos. Risks, 11(3), 49.
Binde, P. (2008). Exploring the impact of gambling advertising. Int. J. Mental Health and Addiction, 7(4), 541–554.
Brown, S. H. V. (2021). Gambling on the blockchain. Vanderbilt J. Entertainment & Technology Law, 24, 535.
Davies, N. & Ferris, S. (2022). Cryptocurrency and new financial instruments. The Lancet Public Health, 7(8).
Deans, E. G., et al. (2017). The influence of marketing on sports betting attitudes. Harm Reduction Journal, 14(1).
Lopez-Gonzalez, H. & Griffiths, M. D. (2023). Gambling-like features in fan tokens. Journal of Gambling Studies, 39(4), 1579–1596.
Macey, J. & Hamari, J. (2022). Gamblification: A definition. New Media & Society.
Mills, D. J. & Nower, L. (2019). Preliminary findings on cryptocurrency trading among regular gamblers. Addictive Behaviors, 92, 136–140.
Newall, P. W. & Weiss-Cohen, L. (2022). The gamblification of investing. Int. J. Environmental Research and Public Health, 19(9), 5391.
Torrance, J., et al. (2023). Gambling, cryptocurrency, and financial trading sponsorship in high-level men’s soccer leagues. Gambling Law Review.
Postcolonial Studies

The CFA Franc and the Long Shadow of Empire: Is France Still Controlling Its Former African Colonies?

Giulio Leonardo Battista · Academic Paper · 2024

For many African countries, political independence arrived in the 1960s. Yet economic independence has been far more elusive. One of the most controversial symbols of this continuing dependency is the CFA franc, a currency still used today by 14 countries in West and Central Africa.

Originally created in 1945 as the Franc of the French Colonies in Africa, the CFA franc has survived decolonisation and continues to tie the monetary systems of former French colonies closely to Paris.

The Mechanics of Monetary Control

The CFA franc zone includes 14 African countries, most of which are former French colonies, divided into two monetary unions with their own central banks: the BCEAO in West Africa and the BEAC in Central Africa.

The CFA franc is pegged to the euro at a fixed exchange rate — currently 1 euro to 655 CFA francs. France guarantees the convertibility of the currency. In exchange, however, member countries must deposit around half of their foreign exchange reserves with the French Treasury. Even the production of the currency reflects this dependence: CFA banknotes are printed in France before being shipped to the African countries that use them.

Critics argue that this arrangement effectively limits the monetary sovereignty of the countries involved. For many scholars, the system is part of what has long been called “Françafrique” — the complex political and economic network linking France with its former African territories.

When the Subaltern Cannot Speak

Postcolonial theorist Gayatri Chakravorty Spivak famously asked: can the subaltern speak? Her concept of the “subaltern” refers to marginalised groups whose voices are systematically excluded from decision-making structures.

Regular meetings between French officials and representatives from the two monetary unions are held twice a year. In theory, these gatherings should provide a platform for joint decision-making. In practice, however, critics argue that power remains heavily concentrated on the French side. This imbalance reflects the dynamic Spivak described: institutions may formally include marginalised actors while still preventing them from shaping the decisions that affect them.

A Core and a Periphery

Immanuel Wallerstein’s World Systems Theory divides the global economy into core and peripheral regions. Many CFA countries rely heavily on exporting natural resources and agricultural products. Because their currency is fixed to the euro, they cannot easily adjust exchange rates to make their exports more competitive. As a result, their economies may struggle to diversify or develop domestic industries.

Dependency theory offers a similar interpretation, arguing that the underdevelopment of the Global South often results from historical and structural relationships with wealthier Northern economies.

Military Power and Economic Interests

Since the 1960s, French forces have intervened in African countries more than fifty times. In Niger, for example, French troops were deployed to protect uranium mines operated by the French nuclear company Areva. Such cases have fuelled accusations that military interventions sometimes function as another instrument of influence within the broader Françafrique system.

The Guinea Precedent

Guinea’s first president, Ahmed Sékou Touré, rejected France’s post-colonial arrangements and refused to join the CFA franc system. According to several accounts, French intelligence services responded with an operation known as Operation Persil, which allegedly involved flooding the Guinean economy with counterfeit currency. The episode remains a powerful symbol in debates about France’s postcolonial influence.

A Future Beyond the CFA?

West African governments have proposed replacing the currency with a new regional alternative known as the Eco. Yet progress has been slow, and critics argue that proposed reforms may still leave significant influence in French hands. Colonial empires may have disappeared decades ago, but the economic structures they created often remain deeply embedded in global systems. The legacy of empire has not disappeared. It has simply changed form.

Bibliography

Ahossey, K. (2022). Financial Influence in Terms of Currency Production and Management. South Asian J. Social Studies and Economics, 13(1), 29–41.
Diop, M. D. (2021). French Monetary Neo-colonialism: The CFA Franc. Università Ca’Foscari Venezia.
Kohnert, D. (2022). Francophonie in Sub-Saharan Africa: Post-colonial Dependence or Self-determination?
Nana, C. N. (2024). Currency Relations between the Global South and the Global North. Beijing Law Review, 15(1), 35–53.
Ntongho, R. (2022). The Political Economy of a Monetary Union in ECOWAS. Springer.
Pigeaud, F. & Sylla, N. S. (2024). L’arme invisible de la Françafrique. La Découverte.
Powell, N. K. (2020). The Flawed Logic Behind French Military Interventions in Africa. The Conversation.
Tavelli, A. (2023). Il Franco CFA. Istituto Analisi Relazioni Internazionali.
Tchatchouang, J. (2015). The CFA Franc Zone. Oxford Handbook of Africa and Economics.
Todd, T. (2013). French Special Forces ‘to Protect’ Niger Uranium Mines. France24.
Wallerstein, I. (2020). World-Systems Analysis: An Introduction. Duke University Press.
Sustainability

Saving Food or Saving Money? The Real Drivers Behind the Success of Too Good To Go

Giulio Leonardo Battista · Academic Paper · 2024

Food waste has become one of the most visible sustainability challenges in modern societies. Every year, millions of tons of edible food are discarded across Europe, despite growing awareness of the environmental and ethical implications of waste. In response, digital platforms have emerged as innovative tools to tackle the problem.

One of the most prominent examples is Too Good To Go, a food recovery app that connects consumers with restaurants, bakeries and supermarkets selling unsold food at reduced prices. Yet the popularity of the app raises an interesting question: are users primarily motivated by environmental concerns, or by the economic advantages the platform offers?

Technology and the Promise of Sustainability

The success of Too Good To Go reflects a broader belief that technology can help solve environmental problems. This perspective is often described as a Promethean approach to sustainability — the idea that human ingenuity and technological innovation can overcome ecological limits.

From this perspective, platforms like Too Good To Go represent a promising example of how digital technology can reshape everyday consumption habits. The environmental benefits appear clear. Businesses are able to recover some of the value from unsold food, while consumers gain access to affordable products that would have gone to waste.

The Economics Behind “Saving” Food

While Too Good To Go promotes sustainability, its widespread adoption may be driven just as much by economic incentives as by environmental values. For suppliers, the platform offers a practical way to reduce losses from unsold products. Consumers may also be driven by similar incentives — the app allows users to purchase “surprise bags” of food at significantly discounted prices.

This dynamic reflects a broader debate in sustainability research: do environmentally beneficial technologies succeed because people care about sustainability, or because they align with economic self-interest?

Between Sustainability and Profit

Eco-Marxist perspectives argue that capitalist systems tend to prioritise profit accumulation above environmental protection. From this viewpoint, technological solutions to sustainability problems often emerge because they create new economic opportunities rather than purely environmental ones.

Similarly, economist Milton Friedman famously argued that the primary responsibility of businesses is to increase profits. In the case of Too Good To Go, the environmental impact is undoubtedly positive. Yet the motivations behind participation may still reflect this economic logic.

Understanding User Motivation

Despite the growing popularity of food recovery apps, relatively little research has examined the motivations behind their use. Most existing studies focus on the environmental impact of these platforms. However, fewer studies explore the social dynamics that drive participation.

Understanding these motivations could offer important insights into how sustainability technologies function in practice.

Technology Alone Is Not Enough

Technological solutions may facilitate more sustainable practices, but they do not necessarily change the underlying motivations driving human behaviour. In reality, the success of many sustainability innovations may depend precisely on their ability to combine environmental benefits with economic incentives.

Platforms like Too Good To Go may work not because people suddenly become more environmentally conscious, but because sustainable choices also happen to be economically attractive. In this sense, the app may offer a pragmatic model for addressing environmental problems: aligning sustainability with everyday economic realities.

Bibliography

Dryzek, J. S. (2021). Growth Unlimited. In The Politics of the Earth (pp. 53–72). Oxford University Press.
Friedman, M. (1970). The Social Responsibility of Business Is to Increase Its Profits. The New York Times Magazine, 13, 32–33.
Ho, H. P. & Nguyen, M. (2024). Too Good to Go: An Innovative App for Surplus Food. Palgrave Encyclopedia of Social Marketing.
Huesemann, M. H. (2003). The Limits of Technological Solutions to Sustainable Development. Clean Technologies and Environmental Policy, 5, 21–34.
Vo-Thanh, T., et al. (2021). How a Mobile App Can Become a Catalyst for Sustainable Social Business. Technological Forecasting and Social Change, 171, 120962.
